text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY
FOLLOW-UP OFFICE VISIT — DISEASE PROGRESSION ASSESSMENT AND TREATMENT PLAN CHANGE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [GU Oncology Attending]
DIVISION: Hematology / Medical Oncology — Genitourinary Oncology Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mrs. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with known stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid differentiation, who presents today for a scheduled restaging visit following CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain performed earlier this week. The primary purpose of this visit is to review imaging results and determine next steps given evolving disease on systemic therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Okonkwo has been followed in our clinic since she was diagnosed with metastatic ccRCC presenting with an 11.4 cm right renal mass, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (aortocaval nodes up to 2.8 cm), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. At the time of diagnosis, sarcomatoid differentiation comprised approximately 20% of the biopsy specimen, and PD-L1 expression was high (CPS 55 by 22C3). NGS from the biopsy demonstrated a VHL p.R161* nonsense mutation, PBRM1 splice-site loss-of-function mutation, SETD2 frameshift mutation, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion — a genomic profile consistent with biologically aggressive ccRCC. IMDC risk was classified as intermediate (two factors: Karnofsky <80 at nadir during workup, hemoglobin below LLN at presentation).

She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy approximately 8 weeks prior to starting systemic therapy. Surgical pathology confirmed pT3bN2 disease with 9 of 14 lymph nodes positive, including extranodal extension, and an IVC thrombus level I-II. Post-operatively, she recovered well, with only a brief post-operative ileus.

She initiated first-line immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV q3 weeks and nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV q3 weeks (CheckMate 214 schedule) approximately 9 months ago. Treatment was notable for:
- Grade 2 immune-related colitis at cycle 3, requiring ipilimumab hold and oral budesonide; this resolved with treatment and ipilimumab was eventually completed for all 4 induction doses with a 1-cycle delay.
- Transition to nivolumab (Opdivo) 480 mg IV q4 weeks maintenance after completing 4 induction cycles. First restaging at 3 months showed a meaningful partial response — all four pulmonary lesions decreased by approximately 40-44%.
- Grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism at ~5 months, with TSH rising to 18.4 mIU/L and low free T4. Levothyroxine initiated and titrated to 75 mcg and eventually 100 mcg with TSH normalization; she remains on this indefinitely.

Her second restaging at ~6 months revealed a mixed response: most pulmonary lesions remained stable, but the right lower lobe lesion grew significantly (1.3 cm to 2.4 cm) and a new 1.3 cm right adrenal nodule appeared. A CT-guided biopsy of the adrenal lesion confirmed metastatic ccRCC without sarcomatoid features. She underwent SBRT to the right lower lobe pulmonary lesion (54 Gy in 3 fractions) approximately 1.5 months ago, with nivolumab briefly held during that week and then resumed.

She now presents for the third restaging scan, performed approximately 9 months after starting first-line immunotherapy. The results are reviewed in detail below.

Since the last visit, Mrs. Okonkwo reports increased fatigue compared to earlier in the treatment course — she now rates it a 4-5 out of 10, and she has noted some right-upper-quadrant fullness over the past several weeks. She denies frank jaundice, abdominal pain, fevers, chills, or night sweats. She has had no neurologic symptoms — no headaches, visual changes, focal weakness, or gait disturbance. No hemoptysis or worsening dyspnea at rest, though she does note mild exertional dyspnea with prolonged stair climbing. Appetite is decreased but she is maintaining her weight (reports approximately 1 kg loss over the past month). No new bone pain. Bowel habits have returned to near-normal after the prior colitis episode; currently 1-2 loose stools per day, which she attributes to ongoing residual gastrointestinal effects. No blood in stool.

Current medications include levothyroxine 100 mcg daily (for checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypothyroidism), amlodipine 10 mg daily (for pre-existing hypertension), atorvastatin (for hyperlipidemia), nivolumab 480 mg IV q4 weeks (last dose 3.5 weeks ago), acetaminophen PRN, and ondansetron PRN for nausea.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue (grade 1-2, increased from prior visit), mild decreased appetite, 1 kg weight loss over approximately one month. Denies fever, chills, drenching night sweats.
Respiratory: Mild exertional dyspnea on prolonged stair climbing; no dyspnea at rest, no hemoptysis, no pleuritic chest pain.
Abdominal/GI: Mild right-upper-quadrant fullness or sense of heaviness, no frank pain. One to two loose stools per day (baseline now), no hematochezia, no melena.
MSK/Neurological: Denies bone pain, new focal neurologic deficits, headaches, or visual changes.
Skin: No new rash; prior hand-foot syndrome not a current concern (not on TKI).
Endocrine: No new symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (no orthostasis, no syncope); hypothyroidism well-controlled on current levothyroxine dose.
Cardiovascular: Blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine. No chest pain or palpitations.
GU: Functioning with single remaining kidney; no hematuria. Last eGFR within acceptable range.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: BP 136/86 mmHg, HR 78 bpm (regular), RR 16/min, Temperature 36.7°C, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 74.2 kg (1.1 kg less than last visit)
General: Well-appearing 63-year-old woman, alert and oriented x4, in no acute distress. Mildly fatigued appearing.
HEENT: Sclerae anicteric; no lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary regions on palpation.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; normal S1 and S2; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles. Breath sounds symmetric. No dullness to percussion.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant. No peritoneal signs. No palpable hepatomegaly on exam (though body habitus makes deep palpation moderately limited). Well-healed right flank surgical scar from prior nephrectomy. No palpable splenomegaly. Normoactive bowel sounds.
Musculoskeletal/Extremities: Full range of motion in all extremities. No focal bony tenderness to palpation over the spine, pelvis, or long bones. No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait steady. No focal motor or sensory deficits.
Skin: No rash, no palmar or plantar erythema. Scars from prior procedures well-healed.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 6.8 x10³/mcL (normal differential)
  - Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL (mildly low; previously 10.4 g/dL at cycle 2 — gradual decline consistent with disease burden and/or chronic inflammation)
  - MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)
  - Platelets: 212,000/mcL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  - Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L
  - Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~58 mL/min/1.73m², appropriate for post-nephrectomy single-kidney baseline)
  - BUN: 22 mg/dL
  - AST: 52 U/L (mildly elevated; upper limit of normal 40 U/L)
  - ALT: 48 U/L (mildly elevated; upper limit of normal 40 U/L)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 124 U/L (mildly elevated; upper limit of normal 115 U/L)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normal)
  - Albumin: 3.5 g/dL (low normal)
  - LDH: 278 U/L (mildly elevated)

Thyroid Function:
  - TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (within normal range on levothyroxine 100 mcg; well-controlled)

Urinalysis: Trace protein (1+); no red cells, no casts. (Proteinuria previously characterized — 24-hour urine protein was not clinically indicated to re-measure at this visit given no active TKI.)

Note: AM cortisol checked due to prior steroids — 12.8 mcg/dL (adequate adrenal reserve; no clinical features of adrenal insufficiency).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (current visit — third restaging):

Pulmonary findings: The previously irradiated right lower lobe lesion has decreased and now measures 1.8 cm, consistent with local treatment effect from the recent SBRT — reassuringly, there is no evidence of radiation pneumonitis in the surrounding parenchyma. However, the other three pulmonary lesions have all grown substantially compared to the last scan: the right upper lobe lesion is now 1.8 cm (up from 0.6 cm last scan), the left lower lobe lesion is 1.4 cm (up from 0.5 cm), and the left upper lobe lesion is now 0.9 cm (up from 0.4 cm). These are outside the radiation field and represent systemic disease progression.

Hepatic findings: A new lesion in hepatic segment VI measures 1.6 cm — this was not present on the baseline scan prior to cabozantinib and was not present even at the six-month restaging. This appears hypervascular on arterial phase, compatible with a new metastatic deposit from her ccRCC. No other hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation. No ascites.

Adrenal: The right adrenal metastasis, previously biopsied and confirmed as ccRCC, measures 1.5 cm, slightly larger than the 1.3 cm on prior scan, consistent with slow ongoing growth despite nivolumab.

Retroperitoneum/lymph nodes: Post-surgical changes from prior nephrectomy and RPLND are stable. No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Osseous structures: No lytic or blastic lesions. No suspicious bone findings on this scan.

Overall impression: Systemic disease progression — significant growth of non-irradiated pulmonary lesions, a new hepatic metastasis, and slight increase in the adrenal metastasis. The SBRT-treated right lower lobe lesion appears to have local control. The pattern clearly represents systemic progression on nivolumab maintenance rather than isolated oligoprogression.

MRI Brain with and without Gadolinium Contrast (current visit):
No intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Previously noted mild white matter changes are unchanged. No new intracranial pathology. Brain remains clear of disease.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mrs. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with IMDC intermediate-risk metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features (22%), presenting today with confirmation of systemic disease progression on nivolumab (Opdivo) maintenance therapy.

1. DISEASE STATUS — SYSTEMIC PROGRESSION ON FIRST-LINE IMMUNOTHERAPY
Today's imaging confirms that despite the SBRT achieving local control of the treated right lower lobe pulmonary lesion, the remaining pulmonary lesions have grown substantially (right upper lobe: 3x increase; left lower lobe: nearly 3x increase; left upper lobe: more than 2x increase). More significantly, a new hepatic metastasis has appeared in segment VI. The right adrenal lesion (previously biopsy-confirmed ccRCC) has also grown slightly. This is clearly systemic progression and not a pattern amenable to further local consolidation — there are simply too many active sites progressing simultaneously for local therapy alone to be a viable strategy.

The overall trajectory is: partial response at 3 months on ipi/nivo, then mixed response at 6 months, local SBRT at 7.5 months, and now unambiguous systemic progression at 9 months. She received approximately 9 months of total immunotherapy benefit before progression. This is meaningful but insufficient for long-term disease control, and her high-risk genomic features (CDKN2A deletion, SETD2 mutation, 22% sarcomatoid component) are consistent with the biological aggressiveness of her disease.

Plan: DISCONTINUE nivolumab effective today. No further immunotherapy at this time.

2. SECOND-LINE SYSTEMIC THERAPY — CABOZANTINIB
After discussing the case in the context of available evidence and current NCCN guidelines, the next line of therapy will be cabozantinib (Cabometyx), a multi-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR1/2/3, MET, AXL, and RET. The METEOR trial demonstrated a significant overall survival benefit for cabozantinib versus everolimus in the second-line setting after VEGF-directed therapy, and several datasets now show robust activity after prior immunotherapy — including the CONTACT-03 data and real-world experience. Cabozantinib inhibits MET and AXL, which are pathways implicated in sarcomatoid dedifferentiation, providing additional biological rationale in this patient's setting.

Starting dose: Cabozantinib 60 mg orally once daily (label dose). Given that she does not currently have significant hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea beyond grade 1, or hepatic dysfunction requiring dose modification, starting at the full 60 mg dose is appropriate, with close monitoring.

Counseling provided for expected toxicities:
- Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Most common at weeks 2-6. Counseled to use urea-based cream daily on palms and soles prophylactically, wear well-cushioned footwear, avoid heat and friction. She has no current HFS given she is not on a TKI.
- Diarrhea: Likely to worsen from her current mild baseline. Instructed to use loperamide 4 mg first dose then 2 mg after each loose stool, and to call clinic if more than 4-5 stools above baseline per day.
- Hypertension: She is already on amlodipine 10 mg for pre-existing hypertension. BP is currently 136/86 mmHg — within acceptable range. She will need close BP monitoring q1-2 weeks initially, with the expectation that cabozantinib will likely worsen her BP. Standing orders placed for BP checks and lisinopril initiation if BP exceeds 140/90 on repeat measurements (lisinopril also has nephroprotective benefit in her single-kidney setting).
- Fatigue: Expected; encourage adequate rest and gentle activity.
- Hepatotoxicity: Baseline LFTs are mildly elevated (AST 52, ALT 48), likely reflecting the new hepatic metastasis rather than drug effect at this point. LFTs will be rechecked in 3-4 weeks.
- Hypothyroidism/thyroid effects: She is already on levothyroxine 100 mcg for checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypothyroidism. TSH currently well-controlled. Will continue to monitor — cabozantinib can also affect thyroid function.
- Nausea: PRN ondansetron provided.

Prescription for cabozantinib 60 mg daily sent to specialty pharmacy. Oral chemotherapy teaching provided by the clinic nurse and pharmacist today. Patient verbalized understanding.

3. HEPATIC METASTASIS
The new hepatic segment VI lesion (1.6 cm) is a new site of disease, first appearing on today's scan. The hypervascular appearance on arterial phase is consistent with ccRCC metastasis. In the context of systemic progression with multiple other active sites, pursuing hepatic SBRT or hepatic directed therapy to this single lesion would not be the appropriate strategy — she has concomitant pulmonary and adrenal progression. This lesion will be followed with systemic therapy; we expect cabozantinib will address the hepatic disease as part of systemic treatment.

4. RIGHT ADRENAL METASTASIS
The confirmed right adrenal metastasis (biopsied last month, confirmed ccRCC) measures 1.5 cm — slight increase from 1.3 cm. Although SBRT to the adrenal was discussed at the MDT earlier this month, the emergence of new hepatic disease and the systemic progression across multiple pulmonary sites argues against local adrenal therapy in isolation. This will be managed as part of systemic therapy. We will re-evaluate in 3 months whether any local strategy for the adrenal metastasis is warranted depending on cabozantinib response.

5. ANEMIA
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, normocytic — mild anemia of chronic disease/cancer. No evidence of active bleeding. Iron studies and reticulocyte count to be checked at the next lab draw. No indication for transfusion at this time. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not appropriate in this setting; will monitor. Fatigue is multifactorial (disease burden, anemia, prior treatment).

6. MILDLY ELEVATED LIVER FUNCTION TESTS
AST 52, ALT 48, alkaline phosphatase 124 — all mildly elevated above normal. In the context of a new hepatic metastasis, this is most likely tumor-related. There is no clinical picture of hepatocellular injury pattern (bilirubin normal, no jaundice). Checkpoint inhibitor hepatitis is a consideration, though she received her last nivolumab dose over 3 weeks ago and the trend from prior labs (which were normal) to now coincides with appearance of the new hepatic lesion, making metastatic disease the more likely etiology. We will recheck LFTs in 3-4 weeks after starting cabozantinib; if LFTs worsen beyond grade 2 on the new agent, further workup (imaging review, possible liver biopsy) will be considered.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM — STABLE
TSH 2.4 mIU/L on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily — well-controlled. No changes to thyroid management today. Levothyroxine to be continued indefinitely given checkpoint inhibitor-associated thyroid destruction.

8. HYPERTENSION
BP 136/86 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg — at the upper acceptable range at baseline before starting cabozantinib. As noted above, will monitor closely and anticipate need for additional antihypertensive therapy. Standing orders for lisinopril 10 mg if BP rises above 140/90 mmHg on repeat measurement.

9. ECOG PERFORMANCE STATUS
ECOG PS 1. Patient is ambulatory, capable of self-care, and active for >50% of waking hours. She remains a candidate for systemic therapy and is motivated to continue treatment.

10. GOALS OF CARE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
We had a brief but important conversation today about the trajectory of her illness. Mrs. Okonkwo understands that her cancer has progressed on immunotherapy and that we are moving to a different class of therapy. She is aware that this is metastatic disease and that the goal of treatment is to control the cancer and maintain quality of life, not to cure. She is emotionally resilient but noted she was disappointed to hear the disease had grown. She continues to live with her adult daughter and maintains close family ties. Her primary stated priorities remain: quality of life, maintaining independence, and being present for her family and grandchildren. She is open to palliative care co-management, which remains in place. Social work referral offered — patient declined today but is aware the offer stands.

Return to clinic in 3-4 weeks (or sooner PRN) for toxicity assessment and lab check on cabozantinib. Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months to assess response. MRI brain will be repeated if neurological symptoms develop.

All questions answered. Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and agreed to proceed.

Time spent: 55 minutes (greater than 50% of time in counseling and coordination of care).

Electronically signed by: [GU Oncology Attending]
Co-signed by: [GU Oncology Fellow]
